E_fission.name: energy released per fission event
E_fission.note: U-235 fission releases ~200 MeV per event; mostly as KE of fission fragments

E_fusion.name: energy released per fusion reaction
E_fusion.note: 4H → He + 2e⁺ + 2ν releases ~26.7 MeV (pp chain in Sun)

m_critical.name: critical mass
m_critical.note: Minimum mass of fissile material needed to sustain chain reaction; ~52 kg for U-235 (bare sphere)

k.name: neutron multiplication factor
k.note: k<1: subcritical (chain dies), k=1: critical (steady), k>1: supercritical (exponential growth)

T_fusion.name: temperature required for fusion
T_fusion.note: ~10⁷ K needed to overcome Coulomb repulsion between nuclei (thermonuclear conditions)

Q.name: Q-value of reaction
Q.note: Q = (mass of reactants - mass of products) × 931.5 MeV/u; Q > 0 means exothermic

locked_facts: U-235 + n → Ba-141 + Kr-92 + 3n (typical fission reaction; releases ~200 MeV and 2-3 neutrons)
E = Δmc² — energy released equals mass defect times c²; applies to both fission and fusion
Chain reaction requires k ≥ 1: each fission produces 2-3 neutrons that can trigger further fissions
Critical mass: minimum fissile mass for self-sustaining chain reaction; below this, too many neutrons escape
4¹H → ⁴He + 2e⁺ + 2ν + 26.7 MeV — proton-proton chain powering the Sun
Fusion requires T > 10⁷ K to overcome Coulomb barrier between positively charged nuclei
Energy per nucleon: fusion of H releases ~6.7 MeV/nucleon vs fission of U releasing ~0.85 MeV/nucleon — fusion is more energy-dense per unit mass
Controlled fission: nuclear reactor uses moderator (heavy water, graphite) and control rods (cadmium, boron) to maintain k = 1
truth_statements: Fission splits a heavy nucleus (A > 200) into two medium-mass fragments plus neutrons; fusion combines light nuclei (A < 20) into a heavier one — both release energy because products have higher binding energy per nucleon
The Sun's energy comes from fusion: converting 600 million tonnes of hydrogen to helium every second, with 4 million tonnes converted to energy via E = mc²
A nuclear reactor maintains k = 1 (critical) for steady power; a nuclear bomb requires k >> 1 (supercritical) for explosive energy release
Controlled fusion has not yet been achieved commercially on Earth because sustaining plasma at 10⁸ K while confining it (magnetic or inertial confinement) is extremely difficult
Fission products are radioactive (e.g., Sr-90, Cs-137 with half-lives of ~30 years), creating long-term nuclear waste — a major challenge of fission energy

aha_moment.description: Student sees a side-by-side energy comparison: one U-235 fission gives ~200 MeV, but one H-fusion gives only ~6.7 MeV per nucleon. Yet fusion releases MORE energy per kilogram because hydrogen is 235× lighter than uranium. Per kg: fusion gives ~4× more energy than fission, and ~10⁷× more than coal combustion.
aha_moment.visual_cue: Bar chart comparing energy per kg: coal (3.3×10⁷ J), fission U-235 (8.2×10¹³ J), fusion H (6.3×10¹⁴ J) — each bar dwarfs the previous by orders of magnitude

STATE_1.title: Nuclear Fission — Splitting the Heavy Nucleus
STATE_1.description: Show a U-235 nucleus absorbing a slow neutron and becoming U-236 (excited state). The nucleus deforms, elongates, and splits into two fragments (Ba-141 + Kr-92) plus 3 fast neutrons. Display energy accounting: mass before vs mass after, Δm → 200 MeV released.

STATE_2.title: Chain Reaction and Critical Mass
STATE_2.description: Each fission releases 2-3 neutrons. Show branching tree: generation 1 (1 fission), generation 2 (2-3 fissions), generation 3 (4-9 fissions). For k>1: exponential growth. Show two scenarios: small mass (neutrons escape, chain dies k<1) vs critical mass (chain sustains k=1). Control rods absorbing excess neutrons for k=1.

STATE_3.title: Nuclear Fusion — Powering the Stars
STATE_3.description: Show the proton-proton chain: 4 hydrogen nuclei → 1 helium-4 + 2 positrons + 2 neutrinos + 26.7 MeV. Illustrate why extreme temperature is needed: protons must overcome Coulomb repulsion (positive charges repel). Compare energy density: 1 kg fusion fuel vs 1 kg fission fuel vs 1 kg coal.

STATE_4.title: Interactive Exploration
STATE_4.description: Student controls neutron multiplication factor k to see subcritical/critical/supercritical behavior. Toggle between fission and fusion modes. Adjust number of generations in chain reaction. Challenge: how many generations for 1 mole of U-235 atoms to fission? Calculate total energy output.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Fission and fusion are opposite processes so one releases energy and the other absorbs it
common_misconceptions.correct: BOTH release energy — fission of heavy nuclei and fusion of light nuclei both produce products with higher BE/A. They are not simply 'reverse' of each other. Energy is released whenever products move toward the iron peak on the BE/A curve.
trigger_phrases: if fission releases then fusion absorbs
ek mein energy nikalti ek mein lagti

common_misconceptions.misconception: If fusion powers the Sun, we should easily be able to do it on Earth for clean energy
common_misconceptions.correct: The Sun achieves fusion because of its enormous gravitational pressure and 15 million K core temperature sustained over billions of years. On Earth, we need even HIGHER temperatures (~10⁸ K) because we cannot replicate gravitational confinement, and containing plasma at such temperatures without it touching any material wall is an unsolved engineering challenge.
trigger_phrases: sun mein hota hai toh earth pe bhi hoga
sun does it so can we

common_misconceptions.misconception: Any heavy atom can undergo fission if hit by a neutron
common_misconceptions.correct: Only certain heavy isotopes are fissile (can sustain chain reactions with thermal neutrons): U-235, Pu-239, U-233. The common U-238 is NOT fissile with slow neutrons — it captures them without fissioning. This is why uranium enrichment (increasing U-235 percentage) is necessary for reactors.
trigger_phrases: koi bhi heavy atom split hoga

legend.uranium_nucleus: Large yellow-red cluster of ~235 nucleons representing U-235
legend.fission_fragments: Two medium clusters (Ba and Kr) flying apart after fission
legend.neutron: Small grey dot — free neutron that initiates or is produced by fission
legend.proton: Small red dot — hydrogen nucleus participating in fusion
legend.helium_nucleus: Compact 4-nucleon cluster (2 red + 2 blue) produced by fusion
legend.control_rod: Black bar absorbing neutrons to control chain reaction rate
legend.energy_flash: Yellow starburst indicating energy release at reaction point

concept_relationships.relationship: The BE/A curve is the foundation — both fission and fusion release energy because products have higher BE/A than reactants
Fission products are often radioactive (beta emitters); alpha decay is a natural form of nuclear disintegration
E = mc² connects nuclear energy to photon energy; nuclear reactions release ~10⁶ times more energy per event than atomic transitions
Conservation of mass-energy, charge, baryon number, and lepton number all hold in nuclear reactions
